perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used solely for giving effect to the Business Under Discussion and further that it will not disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, for an y reason or purpose whatsoever, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as stated otherwise herein or as required by law, order, decree, regulation, or governmental agency request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that the Information will be used for the Business Under Discussion and that it will endeavor not to disclose to any person (i) the Information and (ii) the fact that the Information has been made available to the Receiving Party or that the Receiving Party have inspected any portion of the Information, unless the Providing Party has consented in writing to such disclosure or except as required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'solely for giving effect to' with 'for' and 'will not disclose' with 'endeavor not to disclose' weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. The original text creates a strict obligation to not disclose, unless explicit consent is provided, where the modified text introduces a degree of discretion. Removing 'or governmental agency request' creates a potential conflict.
perturbation.law_citation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, SI 2018/597
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, specifically the change from 'will not disclose' to 'endeavor not to disclose,' weakens the confidentiality obligation. This could lead to a breach of the Trade Secrets Regulations if a trade secret is disclosed due to a failure to adequately prevent disclosure. The original wording provides a stricter protection of the providing party's information. The regulation seeks to protect trade secrets and provide remedies where they are inappropriately disclosed or used. By removing the reference to governmental agency requests, the receiving party is unable to follow the law, since the contract does not allow information to be disclosed for legal reasons.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with trade secret regulations. The original text imposes a strict obligation not to disclose information, whereas the modified text uses 'endeavor not to disclose', which implies a weaker commitment and introduces discretion. The removal of 'or governmental agency request' exacerbates this, as it now seemingly prohibits disclosure even when legally required. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018', indicating a legal framework for trade secret protection that the modified text may undermine.

perturbation.original_text: In th e event that the Receiving Party is required by applicable law or regulation or by legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party agrees that it will provide the Providing Party with prompt written notice of such request(s) prior to the required disclosure and the Providing Party shall use reasonable efforts, at its own expense, to seek a protective o rder or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party believes it may be required by applicable law or regulation or legal process to disclose any of the Information, the Receiving Party will attempt to provide notice to the Providing Party prior to any disclosure. The Providing Party may seek a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'is required' to 'believes it may be required' introduces subjectivity. Replacing 'prompt written notice' with 'attempt to provide notice' weakens the notification requirement. The phrase 'shall use reasonable efforts, at its own expense, to seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy' is changed to 'may seek a protective order,' removing the obligation to actively seek protection and transferring the cost burden. This introduces ambiguity about who bears the cost of seeking a protective order and creates a weaker commitment to prevent disclosure.
perturbation.law_citation: CPR 31.17 - Restriction on use of a document or information disclosed
perturbation.law_explanation: The Civil Procedure Rules require certain conduct with respect to disclosure of documents and information. The original text is consistent with the spirit of these requirements by ensuring notice is given and that efforts are made to maintain confidentiality. By weakening the notice obligation and making the protective order discretionary, the modified text could be interpreted as non-compliant with the expected standards of care when disclosing information during legal proceedings in England and Wales. Parties have a duty to make reasonable efforts to maintain confidentiality, and this change could undermine that.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: discovery | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you discovery In civil actions , the discovery process refers to what parties use during pre-trial to gather information in preparation for trial . The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure have very liberal discovery provisions. Before the rules were adopted in 1938, plaintiffs essentially had to prove their case before filing suit . See notice pleading . The Federal Rules changed that such that under the rules' liberal discovery approach, plaintiffs who strongly suspect that they were wronged can file a lawsuit, even if they do not have solid evidence . Additionally, during discovery, they can force the defendant to give them evidence that they can use to build their case. Discovery under the Federal Rules is very broad. According to Rule 26(b)(1) , "Parties may obtain discovery regarding any non-privileged matter that is relevant to any party's claim or defense." The federal rules also provide several tools that can be used to get information from other parties, including interrogatories , depositions , and requests for admission . A party may also compel other parties to give them access to documents, real property , or other things for review or testing. See Rules 26-37 . Complying with discovery rules is particularly difficult and expensive for institutional defendants because it takes time and incurs legal fees . This difficulty is somewhat mitigated by rules allowing defendants to simply grant plaintiffs access to their records, effectively telling them "if you want it, find it for yourself." See Rule 33 . This does not, however, reduce the legal expenses involved in reviewing and responding to discovery requests. Depositions are particularly expensive. In most states, an attorney’s work product is not obtainable through disclosure. This is an unqualified immunity regardless of subject matter. “ Material prepared for litigation ” is not absolutely protected, however. That generally means that work prepared by non-attorneys for the litigation would be discoverable. For example, in People v. Kozlowski, 11 N.Y.3d 223 (2008) , notes from director interviews taken during a law firm’s internal investigation were not protected from a subpoena filed by the defendants. Discovery rules vary by jurisdiction. See: State Civil Procedure Rules . [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS class actions courts criminal law criminal procedure evidence judicial administration legal practice/ethics wex articles civil procedure courts and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligations on the Receiving Party regarding the disclosure of information. The original text mandates 'prompt written notice' and that the Providing Party 'shall use reasonable efforts...to seek a protective order.' The changed text weakens this to an attempt to provide notice and makes seeking a protective order discretionary. This directly contradicts the spirit of maintaining confidentiality and potentially conflicts with standards of care expected during legal proceedings, as suggested by the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets don't directly address this specific scenario, the general emphasis on pre-trial discovery rules and the defendant's obligation to provide evidence (Snippet 2) suggest that procedural rules govern information disclosure. The Perturbation Explanation accurately states that the changes weaken the notification and protective action obligations, and introduce ambiguity about cost burdens.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of such communication the Receiving Party shall destroy promptly all documents furnished and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in who le or in part of such Information. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party shall be entitled to retain that portion of the Information for legal, regulatory or internal compliance purposes, which will continue to be treated as confidential on t he terms hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of such communication, the Receiving Party will endeavor to destroy documents furnished and will try not to retain copies of such Information. The Receiving Party may retain Information for compliance purposes, treating it as confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall destroy promptly' to 'will endeavor to destroy' and 'will not retain' to 'will try not to retain' drastically weakens the obligation to destroy or not retain documents. Removing 'legal, regulatory, or internal' from 'compliance purposes' makes the exception vague and open to interpretation. Removing 'on the terms hereof' weakens the connection to this specific NDA. All of these changes introduce ambiguity about what is expected when the Business Under Discussion ends.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Data Protection Act 2018 requires that personal data be kept for no longer than is necessary for the purpose for which it was processed. By weakening the obligation to destroy documents and broadening the reasons for retention, the modified text could allow the Receiving Party to retain data longer than necessary, violating data minimization principles under the Act. The original text imposes a stricter requirement for data destruction, aligning better with the spirit of the Data Protection Act.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguities that could lead to non-compliance with data protection principles. The original text requires the Receiving Party to 'shall destroy promptly all documents' but the changed text weakens this to 'will endeavor to destroy documents'. This weakening, combined with broadening the reasons for retention by removing 'legal, regulatory, or internal', contradicts the principle that personal data should not be kept longer than necessary, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, and can be inferred from the mention of the Data Protection Act 2018 in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The introduction of 'try not to retain copies' further exacerbates the ambiguity, potentially violating data minimization principles.

perturbation.original_text: Any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement shall constitute a breach that may cause irreparable inj ury, not readily measurable in money, and for which the Providing Party , without waiving any other rights or remedies at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief or other equitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Any breach of this Agreement may constitute a breach that could cause injury, and the Providing Party may consider seeking injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or threatened breach', weakening the statement that this breach will cause 'irreparable injury, not readily measurable in money', making the right to seek remedies discretionary by changing 'shall be entitled to seek' to 'may consider seeking' all introduce substantial ambiguity. The original text provides stronger protection and remedies for the Providing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating a specific statute, this modification impacts the ability to enforce specific performance (injunctive relief). English contract law allows for specific performance as a remedy for breach where damages are inadequate. By making the right to seek injunctive relief discretionary ('may consider'), the modified text weakens the Providing Party's position and could impede their ability to obtain this remedy, especially given the confidential nature of the information which is generally considered as not being readily measurable in money if breached.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the availability of injunctive relief, weakening the Providing Party's position. The original text states the Providing Party 'shall be entitled to seek' injunctive relief, while the changed text uses 'may consider seeking'. This change from 'shall' to 'may' introduces ambiguity, potentially hindering the Providing Party's ability to obtain specific performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines specific performance as 'a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract', noting that it is 'often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach'. By making injunctive relief discretionary, the modified text contradicts the principle that specific performance should be available when monetary damages are inadequate, thus creating a legal contradiction because the ambiguity undermines the enforceability of specific performance.
